pattern: Reversing only half of the digits decides palindromicity without building a string or risking overflow.
hints: A negative number can never read the same backwards because of the leading minus sign.
You do not need the whole reversed number - once the reversed tail is at least as long as the remaining head, you have enough to compare.
Peel digits off the end into a growing reversed value while the head shrinks; stop when head <= reversed, then compare allowing for a middle digit on odd lengths.

edge_inputs.description: single digit is trivially a palindrome
a trailing zero breaks palindromicity unless the value is zero
odd-length palindrome has a lone middle digit
near-palindrome that differs only at the ends

stress.description: large genuine palindromes and near-misses within 32-bit range
stress.generator_python:
def gen(rng, size):
    half = [str(rng.randint(0, 9)) for _ in range(rng.randint(1, 4))]
    half[0] = str(rng.randint(1, 9))
    s = ''.join(half) + ''.join(reversed(half))
    x = int(s)
    if x <= 2147483647 and rng.random() < 0.5:
        return [x]
    return [rng.randint(0, 2147483647)]
stress.note: value stays inside the signed 32-bit constraint
